ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general duck soup entry; herbal variant with Chinese medicinal herbs is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers meat soups and broths broadly; no specific herbal duck soup code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for herbal duck soup as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 400 g (one bowl), providing around 176 kcal, 26.8 g protein, 7.6 g fat, and 776 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Herbal Duck Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database lists Herbal Duck Soup (F11040005) with all core macros and secondary nutrients. Values used directly as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for herbal duck soup. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on analogy with clear meat-based soups and broths, which have very low carbohydrate content (0.1 g/100g) and negligible glycaemic impact. Analogous reference: clear chicken broth / meat soup, GI ~30–35, University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (32 × 0.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.1, confirming negligible GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 194 mg/100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 776 mg sodium, representing about 34% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one bowl and avoid adding soy sauce or salt; request reduced-sodium preparation when ordering, and balance with high-potassium foods such as vegetables to improve the sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is only 0.1 g/100 g with zero sugar and a low estimated GI of approximately 32, resulting in a negligible glycaemic load of 0.1 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Herbal duck soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a lean, low-glycaemic protein source; avoid pairing with white rice or noodles, which would substantially increase the meal's glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.9 g/100 g) and low saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g) with zero trans fat; herbal ingredients including ginger and wolfberry contribute anti-inflammatory polyphenols.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Herbal duck soup is a heart-friendly choice due to its low saturated fat and anti-inflammatory herbal profile; remove duck skin before consumption to further reduce saturated fat intake, and monitor sodium intake across the full day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Duck is lean poultry with low saturated fat; herbal ingredients (ginger, garlic, wolfberry, red dates) provide anti-inflammatory gingerols, allicin, and antioxidant polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Herbal duck soup is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; ensure ginger and garlic are used in material quantities to maximise anti-inflammatory benefit, and remove duck skin to minimise saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Herbal Duck Soup (药材鸭汤, Sup Itik Herba) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese slow-simmered soup made by braising whole duck pieces with a blend of Chinese medicinal herbs including ginger, garlic, wolfberry (goji berries), red dates (jujube), Chinese angelica root (dang gui), and astragalus root (huang qi). The soup is characterised by a clear to lightly amber broth with a savoury, mildly sweet, and earthy herbal flavour. The duck meat is tender and falls off the bone after prolonged simmering. Nutritionally, per 100 g the soup provides 44 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 1.9 g fat (0.6 g saturated), 0.1 g carbohydrate, 0.0 g sugar, 0.5 g dietary fibre, and 194 mg sodium. It is a lean, high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with negligible glycaemic impact (estimated GI ~32, GL ~0.1 per 400 g bowl). The herbal ingredients contribute anti-inflammatory polyphenols and antioxidants, making this soup popular as a restorative and health-promoting dish in Singapore's hawker and home-cooking culture. It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease; sodium should be monitored for those with hypertension. Commonly served as a main dish at lunch or dinner, often accompanied by steamed rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in herbal duck soup?
Is herbal duck soup good for diabetics?
Can I eat herbal duck soup if I have high blood pressure?
What herbs are in Chinese herbal duck soup?
Is herbal duck soup high in sodium?
Is herbal duck soup suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11040005) with confidence 0.95. Exact match to the named dish. Saturated fat estimated from USDA duck meat composition as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; potassium estimated from USDA duck broth analogue. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database using clear meat soup as proxy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for herbal duck soup not reported in HPB Foodo database (F11040005) or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish as of 2026. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero trans fat for minimally processed duck soup per HPB and USDA duck meat data. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source (F11040005); herbal ingredients such as red dates contribute negligible sugar at typical quantities. Saturated fat 0.6 g/100g estimated from USDA FoodData Central duck meat (skin removed) composition adapted to soup dilution; potassium ~150 mg/100g estimated from USDA duck broth analogue. Halal status marked false as duck preparation method and slaughter certification not confirmed for generic record; consumers should verify halal certification at point of purchase. keto dietary tag from input retained as carbohydrate is 0.1 g/100g. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for herbal duck soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026.
